news_summary: Today’s human rights progress includes strengthened efforts against transnational repression, enhanced AI regulations to protect privacy, courageous journalism from conflict zones, and commitments to safeguard children with disabilities.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès enregistrés aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme comprennent le renforcement de la lutte contre la répression transnationale, le renforcement de la réglementation relative à l’intelligence artificielle afin de protéger la vie privée, le journalisme courageux mené dans les zones de conflit et les engagements pris pour protéger les enfants en situation de handicap.
news_summary_es: Los avances actuales en materia de derechos humanos incluyen un refuerzo de las medidas contra la represión transnacional, una mejora de la normativa sobre inteligencia artificial para proteger la privacidad, un periodismo valiente desde zonas de conflicto y compromisos para proteger a los niños con discapacidad.

articles.title: European Parliament Presses for Action to End Transnational Repression
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              The European Parliament chamber in Strasbourg, France, February 11, 2026.
                    © 2026 Serge Tenani/Hans Lucas via Reuters 
          
    


  
The European Parliament in Strasbourg adopted on June 16 its second motion in a year pressing European Union institutions and governments to address transnational repression.Transnational repression encompasses violations used by states to target, silence, or intimidate critics, particularly nationals and former nationals, who are outside their borders and beyond their territorial jurisdictions. In its worst forms, transnational repression includes killings, attacks, and abductions by government operators or their proxies. Other forms of transnational repression—online harassment, surveillance, denial of consular services, harassment of relatives at home, and abusive extradition requests— are harder to document but have a chilling effect.Exiled human rights defenders, journalists, and opposition supporters are particularly at risk. But virtually anyone who fled repression or speaks out against abuses can become a target. The European Parliament lists China, Iran, Russia, and Belarus at the top of states engaged in transnational repression in Europe and globally. But others, including Egypt, Azerbaijan, Türkiye, and Rwanda, have a troubling record. In east Africa and southeast Asia, governments appear to facilitate or turn a blind eye to cross-border repression on their soil, exponentially increasing the harm transnational repression does to human rights and rule of law.In recent years, the EU’s foreign service has boosted its response to transnational repression, with 2025 seeing references in statements to China’s human rights record and in an EU-led resolution on human rights in Eritrea, and sanctions against Iranian officials.The European Parliament’s motions now provide a more ambitious roadmap for the EU to translate into concrete steps to prevent transnational repression, protect people at risk, and act against those responsible.To do so, EU institutions and members should investigate transnational repression cases, including the roles of officials aiding or abetting such acts, frame them as violations of human rights, inform and protect victims or potential victims, and avoid complicity with abusive extradition requests or during asylum proceedings.They should also use foreign policy to ensure states practicing transnational repression face consequences. With its broad diplomatic footprint, the EU can and should speak up without selectivity and make clear that states involved in transnational repression will face political consequences.Finally, the EU should work with states from all regions to ensure an international response focused on protection and prevention and support efforts by the UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image The European Parliament chamber in Strasbourg, France, February 11, 2026. © 2026 Serge Tenani/Hans Lucas via Reuters

The European Parliament in Strasbourg adopted on June 16 its second motion in a year pressing European Union institutions and governments to address transnational repression.

Transnational repression encompasses violations used by states to target, silence, or intimidate critics, particularly nationals and former nationals, who are outside their borders and beyond their territorial jurisdictions. In its worst forms, transnational repression includes killings, attacks, and abductions by government operators or their proxies. Other forms of transnational repression—online harassment, surveillance, denial of consular services, harassment of relatives at home, and abusive extradition requests— are harder to document but have a chilling effect.

Exiled human rights defenders, journalists, and opposition supporters are particularly at risk. But virtually anyone who fled repression or speaks out against abuses can become a target. The European Parliament lists China, Iran, Russia, and Belarus at the top of states engaged in transnational repression in Europe and globally. But others, including Egypt, Azerbaijan, Türkiye, and Rwanda, have a troubling record. In east Africa and southeast Asia, governments appear to facilitate or turn a blind eye to cross-border repression on their soil, exponentially increasing the harm transnational repression does to human rights and rule of law.

In recent years, the EU’s foreign service has boosted its response to transnational repression, with 2025 seeing references in statements to China’s human rights record and in an EU-led resolution on human rights in Eritrea, and sanctions against Iranian officials.

The European Parliament’s motions now provide a more ambitious roadmap for the EU to translate into concrete steps to prevent transnational repression, protect people at risk, and act against those responsible.

To do so, EU institutions and members should investigate transnational repression cases, including the roles of officials aiding or abetting such acts, frame them as violations of human rights, inform and protect victims or potential victims, and avoid complicity with abusive extradition requests or during asylum proceedings.

They should also use foreign policy to ensure states practicing transnational repression face consequences. With its broad diplomatic footprint, the EU can and should speak up without selectivity and make clear that states involved in transnational repression will face political consequences.

Finally, the EU should work with states from all regions to ensure an international response focused on protection and prevention and support efforts by the UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights.
articles.reasoning: The European Parliament's adoption of a motion to address transnational repression represents a significant and concrete step toward protecting human rights on a broad scale. The motion outlines a clear roadmap for preventing abuses, protecting victims, and holding perpetrators accountable, which can positively impact many vulnerable individuals globally. The article provides detailed context on the issue, the actors involved, and the EU's planned actions, meeting the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for stronger action against cross-border repression
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some governments try to control or harm people who criticize them, even if those people live in other countries. This is called transnational repression. It can include serious actions like threats, spying, or even violence aimed at people living far from their home countries.

**What happened** – On June 16, 2026, the European Parliament in Strasbourg voted for a new motion asking the European Union (EU) and its member countries to take stronger steps against transnational repression. This is the second time in one year they have done this. The motion points out countries like China, Iran, Russia, and Belarus as main offenders, but also mentions others such as Egypt and Türkiye.

**Impact** – This motion gives the EU a clear plan to better protect people who are at risk from these attacks. It calls for investigations into cases, helping victims, and stopping unfair legal actions like abusive extraditions. It also pushes the EU to use its political influence to hold governments responsible when they commit these abuses.

**What's next step** – The EU will work on turning this motion into real actions. This includes looking into cases of repression, supporting victims, and cooperating with international groups like the United Nations to create a global response to protect human rights everywhere.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has set a clear path for the EU to fight against harmful actions by governments targeting people beyond their borders and to protect those at risk worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen demande une action renforcée contre la répression transnationale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains gouvernements essaient de contrôler ou de nuire aux personnes qui les critiquent, même si ces personnes vivent dans d’autres pays. Cela s’appelle la répression transnationale. Cela peut inclure des menaces, de la surveillance ou même de la violence contre des personnes vivant loin de leur pays d’origine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 16 juin 2026, le Parlement européen à Strasbourg a adopté une nouvelle motion demandant à l’Union européenne (UE) et à ses États membres de prendre des mesures plus fortes contre la répression transnationale. C’est la deuxième fois en un an qu’il le fait. La motion cite des pays comme la Chine, l’Iran, la Russie et la Biélorussie comme principaux responsables, mais mentionne aussi d’autres pays comme l’Égypte et la Turquie.

**Impact** – Cette motion donne à l’UE un plan clair pour mieux protéger les personnes exposées à ces attaques. Elle demande des enquêtes, de l’aide aux victimes et la fin des actions juridiques abusives comme les extraditions injustes. Elle encourage aussi l’UE à utiliser son influence politique pour tenir les gouvernements responsables.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UE va travailler pour transformer cette motion en actions concrètes. Cela comprend l’examen des cas de répression, le soutien aux victimes, et la coopération avec des organisations internationales comme l’ONU pour créer une réponse mondiale en faveur des droits humains.

**Une phrase clé** – Le Parlement européen a tracé une voie claire pour que l’UE lutte contre les actions nuisibles des gouvernements ciblant des personnes au-delà de leurs frontières et protège ceux qui sont en danger dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa una acción más fuerte contra la represión transnacional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos gobiernos intentan controlar o dañar a personas que los critican, incluso si estas personas viven en otros países. Esto se llama represión transnacional. Puede incluir amenazas, vigilancia o incluso violencia contra personas que están lejos de su país de origen.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de junio de 2026, el Parlamento Europeo en Estrasburgo aprobó una nueva moción que pide a la Unión Europea (UE) y a sus países miembros tomar medidas más fuertes contra la represión transnacional. Es la segunda vez en un año que lo hacen. La moción señala a países como China, Irán, Rusia y Bielorrusia como principales responsables, pero también menciona a otros como Egipto y Turquía.

**Impacto** – Esta moción da a la UE un plan claro para proteger mejor a las personas en riesgo de estos ataques. Pide investigar casos, ayudar a las víctimas y detener acciones legales injustas como extradiciones abusivas. También impulsa a la UE a usar su influencia política para responsabilizar a los gobiernos.

**Próximo paso** – La UE trabajará para convertir esta moción en acciones reales. Esto incluye investigar casos de represión, apoyar a las víctimas y cooperar con grupos internacionales como las Naciones Unidas para crear una respuesta global que proteja los derechos humanos.

**Una frase clave** – El Parlamento Europeo ha trazado un camino claro para que la UE combata las acciones dañinas de gobiernos que atacan a personas fuera de sus fronteras y proteja a quienes están en riesgo en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, open European-style parliamentary chamber with empty silhouetted seats arranged in a semicircle, where glowing chains dissolve into birds flying free above the chamber, symbolizing breaking the bonds of transnational repression, rendered in warm, natural tones of soft browns, muted blues, and gentle golds.

articles.title: Press release - AI Act: EP approves simplification measures and “nudifier” app ban
articles.summary: The simplification measures aim to support companies as they comply with the AI Act, while maintaining the law’s main provisions and risk-based approach.Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer ProtectionCommittee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The European Parliament has given its final approval to the amendment of certain rules within the EU’s as part of the digital omnibus package. The vote passed with 423 votes in favour, 57 against and 174 abstentions.



New deadlines



The legislation postpones the application of certain parts of the AI Act to ensure that the necessary standards and support measures are in place. Obligations on high-risk AI systems will apply:

from 2 December 2027 for stand-alone high risk AI systems;

from 2 August 2028 for AI systems embedded as safety components and covered by EU sectoral legislation on safety and market surveillance.

The law also delays the application of watermarking obligations on AI-generated content until 2 December 2026. By this time, AI-generated content will have to be labelled in a machine-readable way to increase transparency.



Ban on nudifier apps



The law bans AI systems that generate child sexual abuse material or create images, videos and audio depicting an identifiable person’s intimate parts, or sexually explicit activities, without their consent. Providers will not be allowed to place these systems on the EU market, unless they come with adequate technical safeguards to prevent the creation of such material. The prohibition also applies to deployers using them for this purpose.

Companies will have until 2 December 2026 to bring their systems in line.



Reducing overlaps and centralising enforcement



Other changes to the AI Act include:

the removal of overlapping requirements on AI for machinery products by clarifying that they only need to comply with sectoral safety, while ensuring an equivalent level of health and safety;

a clearer definition of what qualifies as a “safety component”, meaning that products with AI functions that only assist users or optimise performance will not automatically face high-risk obligations, if their failure or malfunction does not pose health or safety risks;

the possibility to process personal data where strictly necessary to detect and correct biases, with proper safeguards, in both high-risk and non-high-risk AI systems;

the extension to small mid-cap enterprises (SMCs) of SME exemptions from certain rules to support their growth;

streamlined enforcement of certain general-purpose AI systems within the EU’s AI Office.



Quotes



During the plenary debate, co-rapporteur for the Internal Market and Consumer Protection committee Arba Kokalari (EPP, SE) said: “To all the entrepreneurs and engineers out there, we are pressing the pause button on the AI Act and we are reducing red tape. It must be simpler to build the future’s tech companies in Europe and to stay in Europe, so we can become an AI continent. Do that, and we can ensure our prosperity, our security and our future. We are also getting rid of duplication of rules for machine products and we are reducing reporting obligations to help companies. We are also banning AI systems that generate child sexual abuse material.”

Co-rapporteur for the Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs committee Michael McNamara (Renew, IE) said: “This omnibus was about establishing legal certainty by extending certain timelines while preserving the AI Act's architecture and strengthening protections where possible. We agreed to a limited change on machinery products, with clear safeguards, and secured an outright ban on AI nudification apps. They impact real people, overwhelmingly women, with the purpose of humiliating, degrading and objectifying them. I'm proud that this Parliament fought for the ban, which will enter into force before the end of this year.”



Next steps



Before the law can enter into force, it still needs to be adopted formally by the Council.

Most of the provisions of the AI Act will start to apply on 2 August 2026.



Press conference



Co-rapporteurs Arba Kokalari (EPP, Sweden) and Michael McNamara (Renew, Ireland) will hold a press conference on Wednesday at 10.00 CEST.



Background



The legislation adopted today is part of the seventh omnibus package on simplification proposed by the European Commission on 19 November 2025 (“the digital omnibus”). Parliament is currently working on two other proposals, one amending the data use and data protection laws, and another establishing European business wallets.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Parliament's approval of amendments to the AI Act that include a significant ban on AI systems generating non-consensual sexually explicit content, especially protecting individuals from harmful 'nudifier' apps. This legislative action has broad societal impact by enhancing digital rights, privacy, and protection against abuse, while also simplifying compliance for companies. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights meaningful legal progress with tangible benefits for the general public, particularly vulnerable groups.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament approves AI law changes, including ban on harmful 'nudifier' apps
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Union has been working on a law called the AI Act to control how artificial intelligence (AI) is used, especially to keep people safe. This law sets rules for different types of AI systems based on how risky they are.

**What happened** – In 2026, the European Parliament agreed to change some parts of the AI Act to make it easier for companies to follow the rules and to protect people better. The changes include delaying some deadlines for companies to meet new rules and banning AI apps that create sexual images or videos of people without their permission, especially those called 'nudifier' apps. These apps can create fake images that harm people's privacy and dignity. The Parliament voted with 423 in favor, 57 against, and 174 abstentions.

**Impact** – These changes are important because they protect individuals from AI tools that can create harmful and non-consensual images, which mostly affect women. This ban helps stop abuse and respects people’s privacy. At the same time, the law makes it simpler for companies to follow the rules by removing some overlapping requirements and giving smaller businesses more time and support. This balance helps keep AI safe and fair for everyone.

**What's next step** – The new rules still need to be officially approved by another EU group called the Council before they become law. Most parts of the AI Act will start applying in August 2026, but the new deadlines for some rules will be later, like December 2027 and August 2028. The ban on harmful AI apps will take effect by the end of 2026.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has updated the AI Act to better protect people from harmful AI content while making it easier for companies to follow the rules.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen adopte des modifications à la loi sur l’IA, y compris l’interdiction des applications « nudifier » nuisibles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Union européenne travaille sur une loi appelée loi sur l’IA pour contrôler l’utilisation de l’intelligence artificielle et protéger les personnes, surtout contre les risques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, le Parlement européen a accepté de modifier certaines règles de la loi sur l’IA pour faciliter la conformité des entreprises et renforcer la protection des individus. Ces changements incluent le report de certaines dates limites et l’interdiction des applications d’IA qui créent des images ou vidéos sexuelles sans consentement, notamment les applications « nudifier ». Ces applications peuvent porter atteinte à la vie privée et à la dignité des personnes. Le vote a eu lieu avec 423 voix pour, 57 contre et 174 abstentions.

**Impact** – Ces modifications sont importantes car elles protègent les personnes contre des outils d’IA qui créent des images nuisibles sans consentement, touchant surtout les femmes. Cette interdiction lutte contre les abus et respecte la vie privée. En même temps, la loi simplifie les règles pour les entreprises en supprimant des exigences redondantes et en aidant les petites entreprises. Cela assure une utilisation sûre et équitable de l’IA.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouvelles règles doivent encore être approuvées officiellement par le Conseil de l’UE avant d’entrer en vigueur. La plupart des règles commenceront à s’appliquer en août 2026, mais certains délais sont repoussés à décembre 2027 et août 2028. L’interdiction des applications nuisibles prendra effet fin 2026.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a mis à jour la loi sur l’IA pour mieux protéger les personnes contre les contenus nuisibles générés par l’IA tout en facilitant la conformité des entreprises.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo aprueba cambios en la ley de IA, incluida la prohibición de apps 'nudificadoras' dañinas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Unión Europea está trabajando en una ley llamada Ley de IA para controlar el uso de la inteligencia artificial y proteger a las personas, especialmente de riesgos.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, el Parlamento Europeo aprobó cambios en la Ley de IA para facilitar que las empresas cumplan las normas y mejorar la protección de las personas. Estos cambios incluyen retrasar algunas fechas límite y prohibir apps de IA que crean imágenes o videos sexuales sin permiso, especialmente las llamadas apps 'nudificadoras'. Estas apps pueden dañar la privacidad y dignidad de las personas. La votación fue 423 a favor, 57 en contra y 174 abstenciones.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios son importantes porque protegen a las personas de herramientas de IA que crean imágenes dañinas sin consentimiento, afectando principalmente a mujeres. Esta prohibición ayuda a prevenir abusos y respeta la privacidad. Al mismo tiempo, la ley simplifica las reglas para las empresas, elimina requisitos repetidos y apoya a las pequeñas empresas. Esto mantiene la IA segura y justa para todos.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas aún deben ser aprobadas formalmente por el Consejo de la UE antes de convertirse en ley. La mayoría de las partes de la Ley de IA comenzarán a aplicarse en agosto de 2026, pero algunos plazos se extienden hasta diciembre de 2027 y agosto de 2028. La prohibición de las apps dañinas entrará en vigor a finales de 2026.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo actualizó la Ley de IA para proteger mejor a las personas de contenidos dañinos generados por IA y facilitar el cumplimiento para las empresas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed drawing of a balanced scale with one side holding a stylized AI brain softly glowing and the other side holding a shield emblazoned with the European Union stars, set against a calm background of interconnected circuitry patterns and gentle waves of light symbolizing regulation and protection, rendered in warm, natural tones.

articles.title: Meet Myat Moe Thu, a woman journalist in Myanmar’s conflict zones
articles.summary: “If we don’t report what is happening, the truth disappears."
articles.content:
Exile Hub is one of Global Voices’ partners in Southeast Asia, emerging in response to the 2021 coup in Myanmar, focusing on empowering journalists and human rights defenders. This edited article is republished under a content partnership agreement.

What you see in the frame is a woman holding a camera in a war zone.

What you don’t see is everything beyond it: the sound of military aircraft overhead, the fear of a second strike, and people running for safety.

While others fled, Myat Moe Thu, a recipient of Exile Hub’s Critical Voices Fellowship 2026, moved toward the scene.

A journalist reporting from the border areas between Shan and Kayah (Karenni) States, she documents the realities of war as they unfold. On the day this image was taken, a military jet had just dropped two 500-pound bombs on a civilian village. As residents fled, fearing another attack, she walked into the aftermath to gather the story.

There was no protective gear. No guarantee of safety. Only a decision to bear witness.

Myat Moe Thu did not begin her career in journalism. She was working in marketing when, during a visit to a media outlet, she was asked if she had ever considered becoming a journalist.

She didn’t fully know what the work would demand, but she chose to try, and eventually fell in love with telling stories and sharing information through her writing.

Even before the coup, she began covering armed conflict, gradually building an understanding of the complexities, risks, and political realities on the ground. Since the coup, she has never left Myanmar. Instead, she has moved between areas often referred to as “liberated territories” in ethnic states, continuing her work as a freelance journalist from within conflict zones.

For Myat Moe Thu, journalism is not only about documenting events. It is about responsibility and ethics.

If we don’t report what is happening, the truth disappears. Reporting doesn’t mean taking sides. It means being accurate, respecting the dignity of those affected, and ensuring that the voices of civilians are not distorted or exploited no matter which side they are on. I know not everyone will agree with me on this.

Working in conflict zones requires more than courage. It demands careful judgment: what to report, how to protect sources, and how to ensure that stories do not cause further harm to already vulnerable communities.

Being a woman journalist in war zones brings additional challenges.

Cultural expectations often frame field reporting as work more suited for men. Women are frequently underestimated or discouraged from entering dangerous environments. At the same time, practical realities such as traveling long distances and sharing limited space with male colleagues can require personal compromises that are rarely acknowledged.

Despite these barriers, she continues her work quietly, challenging both societal expectations and structural limitations.

Beyond physical danger, journalists in Myanmar often face misunderstanding even from their own communities. Some people cannot distinguish between journalists, informants, and intelligence agents. Others see journalists as intrusive people who ask too many questions. There is even a saying that suggests covering one’s meal to avoid journalists “finding out too much.”

For Myat Moe Thu, this misunderstanding extends to her personal life. Even family members and relatives struggle to fully grasp the purpose and urgency of her work.

Journalism, she reflects, comes with a cost, distance, isolation, and, at times, rejection.

“I sometimes think even someone who has done something wrong might get a second chance. But for journalists, if people lose trust in you, there is no turning back.”

Beyond the visible risks lies a quieter struggle: mental and emotional exhaustion.

Prolonged exposure to conflict, instability, and trauma creates conditions for burnout. For journalists working in exile, access to support is already limited. For those reporting from inside conflict zones, it is even more out of reach.

There is often no time, space, or resources to seek help. And yet, she continues.

What keeps her going is not the absence of fear but the absence of regret.

She shares, “I know I will not regret choosing what I believe is right.”

For journalists like Myat Moe Thu, the line between professional duty and personal survival is never fixed. It shifts with every assignment, every decision, every risk taken.

And still, she keeps reporting. Because in places where silence is enforced, the act of telling the truth is not just journalism. It is resistance.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the courageous work of a woman journalist reporting from Myanmar's conflict zones, emphasizing the importance of truthful reporting for human rights and resistance against oppression. It showcases significant real-world impact by empowering marginalized voices and promoting freedom of information under dangerous conditions. The story is focused, detailed, and meaningful for a broad audience concerned with human rights and press freedom.
articles.personality_title: A Myanmar journalist risks everything to share the truth from war zones
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since Myanmar’s 2021 military coup, many journalists have fled the country due to violence and danger. Reporting from conflict areas has become difficult but vital to show what is really happening.

**What happened** – Myat Moe Thu is a woman journalist who chose to stay in Myanmar and report from the front lines of fighting between the military and ethnic groups. She received a fellowship from Exile Hub, an organization supporting journalists in Southeast Asia. On one day, she entered a village just after it was bombed, despite the risks and no protective gear, to tell the stories of the people affected.

**Impact** – Her work offers a rare, honest view of the conflict. She reports carefully to protect people’s dignity and safety, showing the human side of war rather than taking sides. As a woman in a dangerous and male-dominated field, she challenges social expectations and helps others understand the truth behind the fighting.

**What's next step** – Myat Moe Thu will continue reporting from conflict zones, risking her safety to keep sharing important stories. Support from groups like Exile Hub may help her and other journalists get training and protection needed to work under these tough conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Myat Moe Thu’s brave reporting from Myanmar’s war zones highlights the power of truthful journalism even in the most dangerous places.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une journaliste birmane risque tout pour révéler la vérité depuis les zones de guerre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis le coup d’État militaire de 2021 au Myanmar, de nombreux journalistes ont fui le pays à cause de la violence et du danger. Reporter depuis les zones de conflit est devenu difficile mais essentiel pour montrer la réalité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Myat Moe Thu est une journaliste qui a choisi de rester au Myanmar et de couvrir les combats entre l’armée et les groupes ethniques. Elle a reçu une bourse d’Exile Hub, une organisation qui soutient les journalistes en Asie du Sud-Est. Un jour, elle est entrée dans un village bombardé, malgré les risques et sans équipement de protection, pour raconter les histoires des habitants.

**Impact** – Son travail offre un regard rare et honnête sur le conflit. Elle rapporte avec soin pour protéger la dignité et la sécurité des personnes, montrant le côté humain de la guerre sans prendre parti. En tant que femme dans un domaine dangereux et dominé par les hommes, elle défie les attentes sociales et aide à comprendre la vérité.

**Prochaine étape** – Myat Moe Thu continuera à reporter depuis les zones de conflit, prenant des risques pour partager ces histoires importantes. Le soutien d’organisations comme Exile Hub pourra l’aider, elle et d’autres journalistes, à obtenir la formation et la protection nécessaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le courage de Myat Moe Thu à couvrir les zones de guerre au Myanmar montre la force du journalisme honnête, même dans les lieux les plus dangereux.
articles.personality_title_es: Una periodista de Myanmar arriesga todo para contar la verdad desde zonas de guerra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde el golpe militar en Myanmar en 2021, muchos periodistas han huido debido a la violencia y el peligro. Reportar desde zonas de conflicto se ha vuelto difícil pero necesario para mostrar la realidad.

**Qué pasó** – Myat Moe Thu es una periodista que decidió quedarse en Myanmar y cubrir los combates entre el ejército y grupos étnicos. Recibió una beca de Exile Hub, una organización que apoya a periodistas en el sudeste asiático. En un día, entró a un pueblo bombardeado, a pesar de los riesgos y sin equipo de protección, para contar las historias de la gente afectada.

**Impacto** – Su trabajo ofrece una visión rara y honesta del conflicto. Reporta con cuidado para proteger la dignidad y seguridad de las personas, mostrando el lado humano de la guerra sin tomar partido. Como mujer en un campo peligroso y dominado por hombres, desafía las expectativas sociales y ayuda a entender la verdad.

**Próximo paso** – Myat Moe Thu seguirá reportando desde zonas de conflicto, arriesgando su seguridad para compartir historias importantes. El apoyo de grupos como Exile Hub puede ayudarla a ella y a otros periodistas a recibir capacitación y protección necesarias.

**Resumen en una frase** – La valiente labor de Myat Moe Thu en las zonas de guerra de Myanmar muestra el poder del periodismo veraz incluso en los lugares más peligrosos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a lone camera resting on cracked earth near scattered remnants of a shattered village, with soft natural tones highlighting a faint silhouette of a woman’s shadow cast forward, symbolizing courage and resilience amid a muted, war-torn borderland landscape.

articles.title: Syrian Government Pledges Protection for Children with Disabilities
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              A girl walks to school at an Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) camp in the northwestern Idlib province in Syria, near the Bab al-Hawa border crossing with Turkey, on January 6, 2023.
                    © 2023 Rami al Sayed/AFP via Getty Images
          
    


  
Syria’s 14-year conflict destroyed vast swathes of the country and killed hundreds of thousands, but it also left behind over 1.5 million Syrians with war-caused disabilities.Syria’s minister of social affairs, Hind Kabawat, acknowledged this dire situation on June 9 at a UN meeting on the rights of persons with disabilities in New York, calling on international partners to scale up support for rehabilitation and disability rights programs.Prioritizing disability rights is a positive signal from a new government still finding its footing, and the needs Kabawat described are real and well-documented: nearly 28 percent of Syrians have a disability, almost double the global average.Human Rights Watch’s 2022 report, “It Was Really Hard to Protect Myself,” found that children with disabilities in Syria faced compounding failures during the fighting: they could not flee attacks safely, they were shut out of schools, and humanitarian programs routinely have overlooked them.One 18-year-old we interviewed in 2022 had lost her leg in an airstrike five years earlier and still had no prosthetic. She told us she often refused to evacuate during attacks because it was too dangerous to run on crutches. A father told us he could no longer afford hearing aids for his daughter after losing his job and home to the war. Of the 34 children we spoke to, only 1 child was attending school at the time.Humanitarian operations largely failed these kids. Children with disabilities were routinely excluded from mainstream programs, received inadequate aid, and lost access to services when funding started to dry up. Aid workers admitted they hadn’t been trained on disability inclusion.Minister Kabawat’s voice on the global stage is exactly the kind of advocacy that can shift attention. Syria’s new government should now make a firm commitment by developing a national disability strategy with concrete timelines that ensures schools are accessible, teachers are trained, and disability inclusion is built into new civil defense and emergency structures.International donors and UN agencies should likewise ensure disability inclusion is built into reconstruction planning and commit to collecting disaggregated data on children with disabilities so gaps in aid delivery are visible and accountable.Minister Kabawat’s call gives reason for cautious hope. International partners should meet it with resources, and Syria’s new government should back it with policy.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image A girl walks to school at an Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) camp in the northwestern Idlib province in Syria, near the Bab al-Hawa border crossing with Turkey, on January 6, 2023. © 2023 Rami al Sayed/AFP via Getty Images

Syria’s 14-year conflict destroyed vast swathes of the country and killed hundreds of thousands, but it also left behind over 1.5 million Syrians with war-caused disabilities.

Syria’s minister of social affairs, Hind Kabawat, acknowledged this dire situation on June 9 at a UN meeting on the rights of persons with disabilities in New York, calling on international partners to scale up support for rehabilitation and disability rights programs.

Prioritizing disability rights is a positive signal from a new government still finding its footing, and the needs Kabawat described are real and well-documented: nearly 28 percent of Syrians have a disability, almost double the global average.

Human Rights Watch’s 2022 report, “It Was Really Hard to Protect Myself,” found that children with disabilities in Syria faced compounding failures during the fighting: they could not flee attacks safely, they were shut out of schools, and humanitarian programs routinely have overlooked them.

One 18-year-old we interviewed in 2022 had lost her leg in an airstrike five years earlier and still had no prosthetic. She told us she often refused to evacuate during attacks because it was too dangerous to run on crutches. A father told us he could no longer afford hearing aids for his daughter after losing his job and home to the war. Of the 34 children we spoke to, only 1 child was attending school at the time.

Humanitarian operations largely failed these kids. Children with disabilities were routinely excluded from mainstream programs, received inadequate aid, and lost access to services when funding started to dry up. Aid workers admitted they hadn’t been trained on disability inclusion.

Minister Kabawat’s voice on the global stage is exactly the kind of advocacy that can shift attention. Syria’s new government should now make a firm commitment by developing a national disability strategy with concrete timelines that ensures schools are accessible, teachers are trained, and disability inclusion is built into new civil defense and emergency structures.

International donors and UN agencies should likewise ensure disability inclusion is built into reconstruction planning and commit to collecting disaggregated data on children with disabilities so gaps in aid delivery are visible and accountable.

Minister Kabawat’s call gives reason for cautious hope. International partners should meet it with resources, and Syria’s new government should back it with policy.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant positive development where Syria's new government publicly acknowledges the plight of children with disabilities caused by war and calls for international support and policy action. This advocacy signals a meaningful step toward improving disability rights and inclusion in a war-torn country, addressing a broad and well-documented humanitarian issue with potential for large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Syria’s new government pledges support for children with disabilities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Syria has been affected by a 14-year conflict that caused many injuries and disabilities. Over 1.5 million people in the country now live with disabilities caused by the war. Children with disabilities have faced many challenges, such as not being able to leave dangerous areas safely or go to school.

**What happened** – On June 9, Syria’s minister of social affairs, Hind Kabawat, spoke at a United Nations meeting in New York. She recognized the serious problems faced by people with disabilities in Syria and asked for more help from international partners. This is one of the first times Syria’s new government has made such a clear promise to support disability rights.

**Impact** – This promise is important because nearly 28 percent of Syrians have disabilities, which is much higher than the global average. Humanitarian groups have found that children with disabilities were often ignored in aid programs. For example, many children could not get medical devices like prosthetics or hearing aids, and almost none were attending school. Minister Kabawat’s statement brings attention to these problems and shows a willingness to improve.

**What’s next step** – The Syrian government is expected to create a clear plan to support people with disabilities. This plan should include making schools accessible, training teachers, and including disability support in emergency services. International donors and UN agencies are also encouraged to help by including disability needs in their rebuilding efforts and collecting better data to track progress.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Syria’s new government has publicly committed to improving the lives of children with disabilities, opening a path for better support and inclusion after years of conflict.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le nouveau gouvernement syrien s'engage à soutenir les enfants handicapés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Syrie est touchée par un conflit de 14 ans qui a causé de nombreuses blessures et handicaps. Plus de 1,5 million de Syriens vivent aujourd'hui avec des handicaps liés à la guerre. Les enfants handicapés rencontrent de nombreuses difficultés, comme ne pas pouvoir quitter les zones dangereuses en toute sécurité ou aller à l'école.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 9 juin, Hind Kabawat, ministre syrienne des affaires sociales, a pris la parole lors d'une réunion des Nations Unies à New York. Elle a reconnu les graves problèmes des personnes handicapées en Syrie et a demandé plus d’aide aux partenaires internationaux. C'est l'une des premières fois que le nouveau gouvernement syrien fait une promesse claire pour soutenir les droits des personnes handicapées.

**Impact** – Cet engagement est important car près de 28 % des Syriens ont un handicap, ce qui est bien plus élevé que la moyenne mondiale. Les organisations humanitaires ont constaté que les enfants handicapés étaient souvent ignorés dans les programmes d’aide. Par exemple, beaucoup n’avaient pas accès aux prothèses ou aides auditives, et presque aucun ne fréquentait l’école. La déclaration de la ministre met en lumière ces problèmes et montre une volonté d’amélioration.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement syrien doit élaborer un plan clair pour soutenir les personnes handicapées. Ce plan devrait rendre les écoles accessibles, former les enseignants et intégrer le soutien au handicap dans les services d’urgence. Les donateurs internationaux et les agences de l’ONU sont aussi encouragés à inclure ces besoins dans leurs efforts de reconstruction et à mieux collecter les données pour suivre les progrès.

**Phrase clé** – Le nouveau gouvernement syrien s’est engagé publiquement à améliorer la vie des enfants handicapés, ouvrant la voie à un meilleur soutien et à plus d’inclusion après des années de conflit.
articles.personality_title_es: El nuevo gobierno de Siria se compromete a apoyar a los niños con discapacidades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Siria ha sufrido un conflicto de 14 años que causó muchas heridas y discapacidades. Más de 1,5 millones de personas en el país viven ahora con discapacidades causadas por la guerra. Los niños con discapacidades enfrentan muchos problemas, como no poder salir de zonas peligrosas de forma segura o asistir a la escuela.

**Qué pasó** – El 9 de junio, Hind Kabawat, ministra de asuntos sociales de Siria, habló en una reunión de las Naciones Unidas en Nueva York. Reconoció los graves problemas que enfrentan las personas con discapacidades en Siria y pidió más ayuda a socios internacionales. Esta es una de las primeras veces que el nuevo gobierno sirio hace una promesa clara para apoyar los derechos de las personas con discapacidad.

**Impacto** – Esta promesa es importante porque casi el 28 % de los sirios tienen una discapacidad, mucho más que el promedio mundial. Grupos humanitarios han encontrado que los niños con discapacidades a menudo fueron ignorados en los programas de ayuda. Por ejemplo, muchos no recibieron aparatos médicos como prótesis o audífonos, y casi ninguno asistía a la escuela. La declaración de la ministra llama la atención sobre estos problemas y muestra disposición para mejorar.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que el gobierno sirio cree un plan claro para apoyar a las personas con discapacidades. Este plan debe incluir hacer las escuelas accesibles, capacitar a los maestros e incluir el apoyo a la discapacidad en los servicios de emergencia. También se anima a donantes internacionales y agencias de la ONU a ayudar incluyendo las necesidades de discapacidad en sus esfuerzos de reconstrucción y a recopilar mejores datos para seguir el progreso.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo gobierno de Siria ha prometido públicamente mejorar la vida de los niños con discapacidades, abriendo un camino para un mejor apoyo e inclusión tras años de conflicto.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, sturdy wooden schoolhouse with a gently sloping roof, surrounded by a protective circle of colorful, interlocking puzzle pieces symbolizing disability inclusion; in the foreground, a winding path made of crutches and prosthetic limbs leads toward the school, set against a soft, natural palette of earth tones and muted blues, evoking hope and resilience without depicting any human figures.
